ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general glutinous rice ball entry; no specific black sesame filling variant with longan soup exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based sweet dish — glutinous rice ball dessert
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated tangyuan code; A0FKP (rice-based sweet dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for tangyuan or glutinous rice ball dessert as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a bowl of approximately 250 g containing three black sesame-filled glutinous rice balls in longan and red date sweet soup, providing approximately 320 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glutinous Rice Ball (Tang Yuan) with Sesame Filling, and Sweet Soup Components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for black sesame tangyuan in longan red date soup. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for glutinous rice flour (FDC ID 169715), sesame paste/tahini (FDC ID 167702), and dried longan/jujube components, weighted by estimated recipe proportions (glutinous rice balls ~60%, sweet soup ~40%). Input record values (energy 128 kcal, protein 2.2 g, fat 4.8 g, carbohydrate 19.2 g, sugar 10.0 g, sodium 6.0 mg, dietary fibre 1.2 g) are consistent with this composition estimate and are retained. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted for regional dessert benchmarking; no exact tangyuan entry found.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Glutinous Rice and Rice-Based Desserts
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black sesame tangyuan in longan red date soup. GI of 86 is estimated from glutinous rice GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (glutinous rice GI ~98, mean; sweet rice-based desserts range 80–98). The soup component (longan, red dates, sugar) contributes additional simple sugars but the overall dish GI is moderated slightly by the fat content of black sesame paste. A conservative estimate of GI 86 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (86 × 48.0 g) / 100 = 41.3, rounded to 41.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.0 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is very low at 6.0 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is negligible at 6.0 mg/100 g and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, the high sugar load (10.0 g/100 g) with minimal fibre may drive insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation per AHA 2018 guidelines, so individuals with hypertension should limit portion to one rice ball and request reduced sugar in the soup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice flour GI ~86–98 combined with 10.0 g sugar/100 g and only 1.2 g dietary fibre/100 g creates a very high glycaemic load of approximately 41.5 per standard 250 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dessert is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high glycaemic index (estimated GI 86), high sugar content (10.0 g/100 g), and low fibre (1.2 g/100 g), which together produce a glycaemic load of ~41.5 per serving and will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to one rice ball and pair with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100 g and trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g; however, high sugar (10.0 g/100 g) and high glycaemic load contribute to triglyceride elevation and endothelial stress.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lipid profile of this dessert is relatively benign (saturated fat 1.0 g/100 g, trans fat 0.0 g/100 g, cholesterol 0.0 mg/100 g); the primary cardiovascular concern is the high sugar content (10.0 g/100 g) and high glycaemic load (~41.5 per serving), which may elevate postprandial triglycerides and promote endothelial inflammation, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined glutinous rice flour and high added sugar (10.0 g/100 g) with fibre below 2 g/100 g may promote pro-inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Refined high-GI carbohydrates and added sugar in this dessert may exacerbate systemic inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals should limit or avoid this dish and choose lower-GI, higher-fibre alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Sesame Glutinous Rice Ball Tangyuan in Longan Red Date Soup (黑芝麻汤圆配龙眼红枣糖水) is a traditional Chinese dessert widely enjoyed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora, particularly during the Lantern Festival (Yuan Xiao) and Winter Solstice (Dong Zhi). The dish consists of three smooth, chewy glutinous rice flour balls filled with a rich, fragrant black sesame and sugar paste, served in a warm, lightly sweetened soup brewed from dried longan (gui yuan) and red dates (hong zao / jujube). The rice balls have a soft, yielding exterior with a dense, nutty, slightly sweet filling that melts on the palate. The soup is delicately floral and sweet with a mild caramel-like depth from the dried longan and a subtle earthiness from the red dates. Nutritionally, a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 320 kcal, 5.5 g protein, 12.0 g fat, 48.0 g carbohydrate, and 25.0 g sugar. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free. Clinically, it carries a high glycaemic concern due to glutinous rice flour (GI ~86–98) and high sugar content (10.0 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g), producing a glycaemic load of approximately 41.5 per serving. It is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. Sodium is very low (6.0 mg/100 g), making it suitable from a sodium perspective for hypertension, though the high sugar load warrants caution. Black sesame provides modest iron (0.6 mg/100 g) and calcium (16.0 mg/100 g). This dish is a culturally significant comfort food and festive dessert in Singaporean Chinese cuisine.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for black sesame tangyuan in longan red date soup exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component entries for glutinous rice flour and sesame paste, weighted by estimated recipe proportions. Input record values are consistent with this estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for glutinous rice. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish (glutinous rice balls with black sesame filling in sweet soup) is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this traditional composite dessert are not available in approved sources as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; consistent with USDA FoodData Central glutinous rice flour and sesame paste entries). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based vegan dessert containing no animal-derived ingredients). cultural.primary_cuisine field in input ('The Noodle Place - Circular Road') appears to be a restaurant name erroneously placed in the cuisine field; corrected to 'Chinese Singaporean'. Input aliases array was empty; comprehensive multilingual aliases have been added. GI estimated at 86 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous data for glutinous rice; glycaemic load per serving = (86 × 48.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 41.3, reported as 41.5.
